planting.note: Sow indoors roughly a month before the frost-free date.

directions: Firm seed lightly into warm mix and keep evenly moist until it sprouts. This tidy, well-branched dwarf holds soft-pink plumes above uniform mounds. Grow in full sun for reliable, low-maintenance bedding color all summer.

planting.note: Start indoors early to allow the big combs time to develop for cutting.

directions: Sow shallowly in warmth and pot on without root disturbance for tall, single-stem plants. Grow in full sun and rich soil, and support the heavy heads if exposed to wind. Cut the large purple brain-combs when fully colored and firm for long vase or dried use.

planting.note: Begin indoors four to six weeks ahead of setting out after frost.

directions: Start seed warm and transplant carefully to keep the main stem growing straight. This tall cut-flower cockscomb produces one large orange-red comb per stem. Plant in full sun with steady moisture, and harvest the combs at full size for the vase.

planting.note: Sow indoors early for very short, container-friendly plants.

directions: Surface-sow into warm cells and grow bright so the dwarf plants stay compact. This miniature crested mix carries surprisingly large combs on tiny plants. Use in small pots or edges in full sun for a bright patchwork of jewel colors.

planting.note: Start indoors about a month before setting out.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep warm and bright to hold the dwarf, early habit. This very short crested series sits its wide combs right atop the foliage. Plant in full sun for uniform, weather-tough bedding with deep mahogany-red heads.

planting.note: Sow indoors four to six weeks before the last frost.

directions: Sow shallowly in warmth and move on gently into full sun. Unlike single-comb types, this award winner branches freely to carry many smaller scarlet combs. It performs well in heat and humidity and blooms steadily from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Begin indoors a month or so before the frost-free date.

directions: Start seed warm and transplant without checking growth. This classic cockscomb bears very large, deep-red combs on sturdy stems. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, and cut the combs at full color for fresh or dried arrangements.

planting.note: Start indoors early for tall, single-stem cut flowers.

directions: Sow warm and grow on one strong stem per plant, spacing tightly for straight, long stems. This series is bred for cut-flower production with uniform, dense combs. Plant in full sun and harvest when the comb is fully formed but before seed sets.

planting.note: Sow indoors four to six weeks before planting out.

directions: Start seed in warm cells and set out single stems in full sun once frost has passed. This Japanese cut-flower cockscomb forms very large, tightly folded combs. Grow with even feeding and moisture, and cut the heads when fully colored for florist work.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors for earlier bloom.

directions: Sow onto warm, well-drained soil and cover the seed barely, thinning to give plants room to branch. This tall wheat celosia shrugs off heat and needs little care. Slender pink spikes fading to silver bloom all season and both cut and dry well.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once soil is warm; it self-sows readily in mild areas.

directions: Barely cover seed on warm ground and keep lightly moist until sprouting. This selection adds purple-flushed foliage under rose-purple wheat spikes. Grow in full sun with little fuss and enjoy continuous bloom that draws pollinators into fall.

planting.note: Start indoors early for a compact, fast-flowering pot plant.

directions: Surface-sow into warm cells and grow bright for stocky, well-branched plants. This dwarf wheat celosia flowers early and stays neat in containers. Plant in full sun for a dense, rounded mound topped with short purple-red spikes.

planting.note: Start indoors early for long, harvest-ready cutting stems.

directions: Sow warm and transplant carefully, spacing tightly to encourage long, straight stems. This is a cut-flower wheat celosia bred for uniform, well-branched plants. Grow in full sun and harvest the terracotta spikes when several florets have opened for the best vase life.

planting.note: Begin indoors four to six weeks before the frost-free date.

directions: Start seed in warmth and set out after frost into full sun and free-draining soil. This tall cut-flower type carries slim magenta-purple spikes on branching stems. It thrives in heat with little input; cut stems as the spikes color for arrangements or drying.

planting.note: Sow indoors early for tall green-spiked cutting stems.

directions: Surface-sow into warm cells and grow on in bright light before hardening off. This wheat celosia is prized for its pale chartreuse-green spikes that work as filler. Plant in full sun and cut when spikes are well formed for a fresh green accent in bouquets.

planting.note: Start indoors early; often grown from cuttings in the trade but seed works.

directions: Sow warm or set out plugs, and grow in full sun with sharp drainage. This compact wheat celosia is exceptionally heat- and drought-tough once established. Bright magenta-purple candle spikes bloom nonstop through summer without deadheading.

planting.note: Start indoors early or plant purchased plugs after frost.

directions: Set plants into full sun and lean, well-drained soil, watering sparingly once rooted. This selection deepens the flower color to a rich dark purple over bronze-tinged foliage. It blooms tirelessly through heat and needs no trimming to keep going until frost.

planting.note: Start indoors early for tall stems suited to cutting.

directions: Sow in warmth and grow on strong before transplanting into full sun. This vigorous wheat celosia produces branching stems topped with pink-to-rose spikes. Cut when the spikes are colored and firm; the stems hold well fresh and dry for winter bouquets.

planting.note: Start indoors early for tall plumes to cut and dry.

directions: Sow warm and transplant without disturbing roots into full sun and fertile soil. This tall plumed mix carries large, airy feathers in warm pastel shades. Cut the plumes just as they reach full size and hang them upside down to dry for lasting arrangements.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; this heirloom self-sows in warm gardens.

directions: Sow seed shallowly on warm soil and thin generously so the big plants can branch. This tall heirloom plume celosia loves heat and tolerates poor, dry ground. Enormous crimson feathers rise on strong stems from midsummer until frost.

planting.note: Start indoors four to six weeks before the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow warm and grow on in bright light to keep the foliage color strong. This tall plumosa sets vivid scarlet feathers above bronze-green leaves. Plant in full sun for bold vertical accents in beds and long-lasting cut stems.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as the ground can be worked, or in autumn in mild regions.

directions: Scatter seed about a quarter inch deep and cover lightly, since darkness aids germination. Thin seedlings to a hand's width apart and pinch young tips to encourage branching. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to keep the classic blue blooms coming from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Sow directly into the garden in early spring or autumn where winters are mild.

directions: Press seed a quarter inch into well-drained soil and keep evenly moist until sprouts appear. Stake the tall stems in exposed sites and cut flowers often for the vase. Fully double, deep cobalt-blue blooms open across summer and dry well for everlasting arrangements.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring, or in autumn in mild climates for earlier, sturdier plants.

directions: Sow shallowly and firm the soil over the seed, then thin the seedlings once they have a few true leaves. Give the tall stems a little support and deadhead to prolong flowering. The velvety, near-black maroon flowers are prized by florists and hold their color when dried.

planting.note: Sow direct in early spring for compact plants suited to the front of a bed.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep and thin the compact seedlings to a few inches apart. This tidy, well-branched selection needs no staking and blooms freely without much fuss. Expect masses of rich double-blue flowers on neat mounds through the summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring once the soil is workable, or in fall in mild areas.

directions: Set seed lightly under a quarter inch of soil and keep the bed moist until germination. Thin generously and support the tall stems where wind is a problem. Unusually large, fully double deep-blue heads make this one a standout for cutting.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or autumn for a long succession of bloom.

directions: Cover seed with a quarter inch of fine soil and thin the seedlings to give each room to branch. Deadhead often and cut freely for bouquets. This blend produces frosted, contrasting-tipped petals in blue, rose, wine, and white shades.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring; the compact plants are ideal for edging and containers.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin the dwarf seedlings to a few inches apart. These bushy little plants rarely need staking and bloom quickly from seed. Look for a cheerful mix of blue, pink, rose, red, and white double flowers.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or autumn; this old variety thrives from a hardy start.

directions: Scatter seed thinly and cover with about a quarter inch of soil. This heirloom bears the single, true-blue flowers of the classic wild cornflower on tall wiry stems. Deadhead to extend the display and leave a few heads to self-sow for next year.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring or fall for pure white blooms.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep and thin once the seedlings are established. Support the tall stems and cut often to keep new buds forming. Clean white double flowers make an elegant partner to the blue forms in the border and the vase.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or autumn where winters stay mild.

directions: Press seed lightly under a quarter inch of soil and keep moist until sprouting. Thin the seedlings and deadhead spent heads to prolong bloom. Deep rosy-red double flowers add warm color to the traditional cornflower palette.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring, or in fall in mild-winter gardens.

directions: Cover seed with a quarter inch of soil and thin seedlings to a hand's width apart. Pinch early to build bushy plants and cut flowers regularly for the house. Soft pink double blooms mix beautifully with the blue and white forms.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or autumn for the darkest flower color.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep, firm the soil, and thin once seedlings establish. Stake the tall stems and remove faded heads to keep bloom going. The deep chocolate-maroon flowers are among the darkest of any cornflower and dry well.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring or fall for soft lavender-mauve tones.

directions: Set seed a quarter inch deep and thin the seedlings to give room to branch. Deadhead through the season and cut stems for informal bouquets. Gentle mauve double flowers soften a mixed cornflower planting.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or autumn for crisp white blooms.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep the bed moist until seedlings appear. Thin them out and support tall growth where needed. Snow-white double flowers brighten borders and pair cleanly with any color.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring or fall; a strong cut-flower selection.

directions: Cover seed with a quarter inch of soil and thin the seedlings once established. The long, straight stems make this a favorite for cutting and drying. Rounded, fully double true-blue heads bloom over a long summer season.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or autumn for tall, cut-flower stems.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings to stand a hand's width apart. Cut stems just as the buds open for the longest vase life. Deep wine-red double flowers sit atop sturdy stems all summer.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring or fall for soft pink cutting flowers.

directions: Press seed a quarter inch into the soil and thin seedlings as they grow. Deadhead often and harvest stems for the vase to keep new buds forming. Clear pink double blooms add a gentle note to the border.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or autumn for lavender-mauve cut flowers.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin to give plants room to branch. Support tall stems in windy spots and cut regularly. Soft mauve double flowers blend well in a pastel cutting garden.

planting.note: Sow direct in early spring or fall for clean white blooms.

directions: Cover seed with a quarter inch of soil and keep moist until sprouts show. Thin the seedlings and cut stems freely for arrangements. Pure white double flowers offer a crisp contrast among colored cornflowers.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring; this compact strain suits pots and edging.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep and thin the dwarf seedlings a few inches apart. These early, tidy plants flower quickly and need no staking. Bright blue double blooms cover neat mounds from early summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring for a low, early-flowering edging plant.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin the compact seedlings for good airflow. The dwarf habit stays upright without support and blooms early from seed. Soft pink double flowers make a charming front-of-border ribbon.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring; compact and container-friendly.

directions: Cover seed lightly and thin the dwarf seedlings to a few inches apart. These low plants flower early and stay neat all season. Clean white double blooms brighten edging and mixed containers.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring for a dwarf, early-blooming red form.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep and thin the compact seedlings once established. The short, sturdy plants flower quickly and need no staking. Rosy-red double blooms give the dwarf series a warm accent.

planting.note: Sow direct in early spring or autumn for a bicolor cottage blend.

directions: Scatter seed thinly, cover a quarter inch, and thin the seedlings once they are up. Deadhead and cut often to keep this cottage-garden blend flowering. Expect a soft, romantic mix of blue, rose, wine, and white shades.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall for a full range of cornflower colors.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep and thin the seedlings to a hand's width apart. Support the tall stems and deadhead to keep the succession going. This classic blend delivers double flowers in blue, pink, red, mauve, and white.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring; one of the shortest cornflowers, ideal for pots.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin the tiny seedlings to a few inches apart. This very compact form is perfect for containers, edging, and windy sites. Bright blue flowers smother the low cushions through summer.

planting.note: Direct sow when frost danger has passed and nights stay mild.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil and keep evenly moist until seedlings appear. This selection carries deep violet-purple heads on tall spiny stems that need room at the back of a bed. Bloom continues all summer, and seed pods can be left to reseed or clipped to tidy the plant.

planting.note: Direct sow into warmed soil after frost, or transplant started seedlings.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep the bed warm until sprouts emerge in a couple of weeks. Rich cherry-rose heads glow on tall stems and draw hummingbirds and bees through the hottest months. Water during dry spells, but avoid rich feeding that produces floppy growth.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after the last spring frost when soil is warm.

directions: Sow seed on the soil surface and firm it in, keeping the bed moist until germination. Clean white flower heads brighten evening borders and pair well with darker companions. Blooms hold from midsummer to frost and reseed if pods are left to ripen.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors four to six weeks early.

directions: Scatter seed thinly on warm soil and press lightly, thinning crowded seedlings as they grow. Soft pink heads stand tall on airy stems and shrug off heat and poor soil. Enjoy a long summer show and clip a few seed pods for drying or reseeding.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last frost date.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep warm and moist until sprouting begins. Deep purple-pink flower heads top spiny stalks that make a bold statement at the rear of a border. Water in droughts, deadhead to extend bloom, and let a few pods self-sow.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when the ground has warmed.

directions: Cover seed barely and provide a warm, bright spot for germination. Muted mauve-pink heads bring a softer tone to summer borders on tall, self-supporting stems. Leave spent pods to reseed or remove them to keep the planting neat.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost, or start indoors and transplant when warm.

directions: Surface-sow this heirloom and keep it warm, as fluctuating temperatures aid germination. Pure white flower heads open on tall stems and glow at dusk in the evening garden. It blooms tirelessly through summer and self-sows dependably where pods are allowed to ripen.

planting.note: Start indoors about a month before the last frost, then transplant.

directions: Start seed indoors in warmth and set young plants out after frost. This compact, well-branched selection holds pale pink heads that deepen with age on a shorter, tidier plant. Its bushy habit needs little staking and it flowers heavily from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors about five weeks ahead, transplant after frost.

directions: Germinate seed warm indoors and harden seedlings before planting out. This dwarf Sparkler forms a compact bush topped with rich rose-pink heads that need no staking. Bloom starts early and holds through summer, drawing bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Start indoors before the last frost and transplant when warm.

directions: Sow indoors in warmth and move sturdy seedlings out after the last frost. Crisp white heads sit atop a compact, branching frame that keeps its shape without support. It flowers freely all season and mixes well with the other Sparkler colors.

planting.note: Start indoors roughly a month early, transplant after frost.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and plant out once nights are reliably mild. Soft lavender heads crown this compact, bushy selection that fits smaller beds and large containers. Deadheading is optional; the plant blooms steadily from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after the last frost; this sterile hybrid is not grown from seed.

directions: Plant nursery-grown transplants after frost in full sun and well-drained soil. This thornless, scentless hybrid produces continuous lavender-pink blooms without setting seed or reseeding, so it stays where you plant it. It needs no deadheading and flowers nonstop until frost.

planting.note: Transplant rooted plants after frost; propagated from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Set transplants in a sunny, well-drained spot after the danger of frost passes. This thornless, non-reseeding hybrid opens white blooms that age to the palest pink for a soft, clean look. It carries on without deadheading from planting until frost.

planting.note: Plant rooted transplants after frost; a sterile cutting-grown hybrid.

directions: Plant transplants after frost in full sun with good drainage. This thornless hybrid produces vivid pink flowers on a bushy, upright plant that never sets seed or spreads. It blooms without pause and needs no cleanup through the season.

planting.note: Start indoors before frost or direct sow once soil is warm.

directions: Sow warm and transplant after frost into a sunny bed or container. This compact, nearly thornless selection stays low and tidy while producing pink spider blooms. Its smaller size suits edging and pots, and it flowers steadily through the heat of summer.

planting.note: Start indoors about five weeks before the last frost.

directions: Germinate seed indoors in warmth and set plants out after frost. This blend gives a mix of blush, rose, white, and lavender heads on compact, self-supporting plants. Expect a full season of bloom from early summer to first frost.

planting.note: Direct sow near the last frost; fall sowing supplies cold stratification.

directions: Scatter seed on lean, well-drained soil and rake in lightly; chilling over winter aids germination. This arid-land native carries bright yellow spider heads that pollinators favor and needs little water once up. It blooms in summer and reseeds where soil stays open.

planting.note: Sow in warm soil after frost; it thrives in hot weather.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm, fertile soil and keep moist until seedlings appear. Grown across Africa and Asia as a leafy vegetable, its young shoots are edible while the white long-stamened flowers feed pollinators. Pinch tips to encourage leaf harvest, or let plants bloom and set seed in the heat.

planting.note: Start indoors about a month before frost, transplant when warm.

directions: Raise seedlings warm indoors and plant out after frost in full sun. This compact selection carries soft white blooms that pick up a pale pink blush as they age. Its bushy, well-branched habit needs little support and blooms from summer to frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors ahead of frost and transplant into warm soil.

directions: Start seed indoors in warmth and set out sturdy plants after the last frost. Deep violet-purple heads sit on a compact, branching frame suited to borders and large pots. It flowers continuously through summer with little care.

planting.note: Direct sow into cool soil in very early spring, or in autumn where winters are mild.

directions: Sow seed about an eighth of an inch deep directly where plants are to grow, since seedlings dislike being moved. Chill the seed in the refrigerator for a week or two before sowing and press it into cool soil, as warmth above sixty-five degrees suppresses sprouting. Thin young plants to a hand's width apart and cut spikes for the vase when the lowest few florets open. All parts are poisonous, so keep seed away from children and livestock.

planting.note: Scatter seed on cool bare ground in early spring or the previous autumn.

directions: Broadcast the seed thinly over cool, worked soil and rake it in lightly, no more than an eighth of an inch deep. This airy, wiry-stemmed species self-sows freely and tolerates lean, dry ground once established. Thin to a few inches apart and enjoy the loose branching sprays of violet-blue flowers from early summer. Every part of the plant is toxic if eaten.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool early-spring soil or in fall in mild-winter regions.

directions: Press seed lightly into cool soil where it is to flower, covering only shallowly. This species carries dense spikes of purple-violet blooms on modest stems and adapts to poorer soils. Thin seedlings so air moves freely between them and stake taller plants in exposed spots. Handle with care, as the foliage and seeds are poisonous.

planting.note: Sow directly into cool soil early in spring or in autumn where winters stay mild.

directions: Sow shallowly in place, barely covering the seed, and give a cold spell before or after sowing to break dormancy. This classic strain produces tall, well-branched spikes of fully double flowers in a blend of blue, pink, rose, lavender, and white. Thin to a hand's width and support the stems in windy gardens. Cut when a third of the florets are open for long-lasting bouquets.

planting.note: Direct sow into chilled early-spring soil, or fall-sow in mild climates.

directions: Sow the seed an eighth of an inch deep directly in the bed, chilling it beforehand for more even sprouting. This unusual selection opens smoky slate-grey to gunmetal flowers prized by flower arrangers. Thin plants to give each room, and stake the slender spikes early. Cut stems when the bottom blooms open, or leave them to form decorative seed pods; all parts are poisonous.

planting.note: Sow directly in cool spring soil or the prior autumn in mild regions.

directions: Barely cover the seed in cool, well-drained soil where the plants will flower. This bicolor bears soft blue blooms brushed and edged with white for a frosted effect. Thin to a hand's width, keep evenly watered, and stake before spikes lengthen. Harvest for the vase as the lower florets unfurl.

planting.note: Direct sow into cool soil in early spring, or in fall where winters are gentle.

directions: Press the pre-chilled seed lightly into cool soil in its final position. This moody selection carries dusky purple-grey flowers with darker smudged centers, lovely against silver foliage. Thin the seedlings, stake the stems, and water at the base to keep foliage dry. Cut spikes for arrangements once the first blooms open.

planting.note: Sow directly in cool early-spring soil or the previous autumn in mild zones.

directions: Sow shallowly in place and chill the seed first for quicker, more even germination. Tall spires open in soft lavender and dusky mauve shades that blend gently in borders and bouquets. Thin plants to a hand's width and stake the stems while young. Snip for the vase when the lowest flowers begin to open.

planting.note: Direct sow into cool soil in early spring or fall-sow in mild-winter areas.

directions: Sow the seed thinly and shallowly where it is to bloom, keeping the soil cool and moist. This compact strain forms fat, hyacinth-like spikes densely packed with double florets in mixed pastel shades. Thin seedlings and support stems only if the site is windy. Cut when a quarter of the spike has opened for tidy, long-lasting stems.

planting.note: Sow directly in cool early-spring soil or the prior fall in mild regions.

directions: Scatter seed over cool, worked soil and cover only lightly. This short, bushy mix in mixed colors suits the front of a bed or a large container and rarely needs staking. Thin the seedlings a few inches apart to encourage branching. Deadhead spent spikes to prolong the display through early summer.

planting.note: Direct sow into cool soil early in spring, or fall-sow in mild climates for cutting.

directions: Sow shallowly in place after chilling the seed, keeping the bed cool and evenly moist. Part of a cutting series bred for long, uniform stems, this one blooms in clear rose-pink. Space closely for straight single stems and net or stake the planting. Cut when the bottom third of the spike is open for the vase.

planting.note: Sow directly into cool early-spring soil, or in autumn in mild-winter regions.

directions: Chill the seed, then press it lightly into cool soil where it is to grow. This cut-flower selection sends up tall stems topped with deep, saturated blue florets. Grow it close together with support netting for the straightest spikes. Harvest as the lowest few flowers open and strip the lower leaves before conditioning in water.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool spring soil or fall-sow where winters are mild.

directions: Sow the pre-chilled seed shallowly in cool soil in its flowering spot. This member of the cutting series produces soft sky-blue spikes on long, sturdy stems. Space plants closely and provide horizontal netting to keep stems upright. Cut for arrangements when the bottom blooms just begin to open.

planting.note: Sow directly into cool early-spring soil, or autumn-sow in mild climates.

directions: Press chilled seed lightly into cool soil where the plants are to bloom. Bred for cutting, this one carries tall stems of soft lilac-lavender florets. Grow closely spaced with netting for long straight spikes suited to bouquets. Cut when a third of the spike has opened and keep the toxic foliage away from pets.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool spring soil or fall-sow in mild-winter areas.

directions: Sow shallowly in cool soil after a chilling period to encourage even sprouting. This cutting-series color opens rich carmine to deep rose-red on tall, straight stems. Plant closely and net the bed so the spikes stay upright. Harvest when the lowest florets open for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Sow directly into cool early-spring soil, or in autumn where winters are gentle.

directions: Barely cover the chilled seed in cool soil where it is to flower. This cutting selection produces crisp pure-white spikes valued for wedding work and mixed bouquets. Space plants close together with netting for tall, unbranched stems. Cut when the bottom flowers open and condition promptly in clean water.

planting.note: Direct sow into cool spring soil or fall-sow in mild climates for tall cut stems.

directions: Sow the pre-chilled seed shallowly in cool soil in its final spot. This vigorous cutting series reaches good height with densely filled spikes of deep blue. Grow it close with support netting to keep the long stems straight. Cut when the lowest florets open for premium bouquet material.

planting.note: Sow directly in cool early-spring soil or the previous autumn in mild regions.

directions: Press chilled seed lightly into cool soil where plants are to flower. This tall cutting selection carries full spikes in a soft lilac shade. Space closely and net the planting so stems grow long and upright. Harvest as the bottom of the spike opens for long-lasting cut flowers.

planting.note: Direct sow into cool spring soil or fall-sow in mild-winter areas.

directions: Sow shallowly in cool soil after chilling the seed for a week or two. This cutting selection opens in a warm salmon-pink that is uncommon among larkspurs. Grow plants close together with netting for long, straight stems. Cut when the lowest flowers begin to open and remove the lower leaves.

planting.note: Direct sow into cool early-spring soil, or in autumn where winters are mild.

directions: Sow thinly and shallowly in cool, worked soil where the plants will bloom. This branching selection forms an airy haze of small, clear blue flowers loved by pollinators and arrangers. Thin the seedlings to give each room to spread and let some self-sow for next year. Cut the wispy stems for filler when the first flowers open; all parts are toxic if eaten.

planting.note: Sow directly in cool spring soil or fall-sow in mild-winter regions.

directions: Sow the chilled seed an eighth of an inch deep in its final position. This upright color form carries tall spikes densely set with deep blue double flowers. Thin to a hand's width and stake before the stems reach full height. Cut for arrangements as the lowest florets open.

planting.note: Direct sow into cool early-spring soil or the previous autumn in mild climates.

directions: Press pre-chilled seed lightly into cool soil where plants are to grow. This tall color selection opens rich carmine-rose double flowers along stately spikes. Thin the seedlings and support the stems early in exposed beds. Snip for the vase when a third of the spike is in bloom.

planting.note: Sow directly in cool spring soil or fall-sow in mild-winter areas.

directions: Sow the chilled seed shallowly in cool soil in its flowering spot. This tall selection produces clean white double spikes that brighten borders and bouquets alike. Thin plants to a hand's width and stake the stems before they elongate. Cut when the bottom flowers open for the vase.

planting.note: Direct sow into cool early-spring soil or the prior autumn in mild regions.

directions: Sow shallowly in cool soil after chilling the seed for even germination. This tall selection carries double spikes in a warm salmon-pink shade. Thin the seedlings and provide support in windy sites. Harvest when the lowest florets open and keep the poisonous plant parts away from children and animals.

planting.note: Sow directly outdoors once frost danger has passed and the soil has warmed.

directions: Scatter seed and barely cover it, about a quarter inch deep, in lean soil that is not overly rich. Thin seedlings and avoid heavy feeding, which produces foliage at the expense of flowers, then stake tall stems in windy sites. Deadhead spent daisies to keep the airy, feathery plants blooming from midsummer until frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost into warm, well-drained ground.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil about a quarter inch deep and keep evenly moist until sprouts appear. This heat-loving species tolerates poor soil and drought once established, rewarding neglect with hot orange and gold blooms. Pinch young plants to encourage branching and remove faded flowers to prolong the display into autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers after frost, or set out started plants once nights stay warm.

directions: Set the tuber a few inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and water sparingly until growth begins. Prized for velvety maroon flowers that carry a warm chocolate fragrance strongest on sunny afternoons. In cold regions lift the tubers before hard frost and store them dry and frost-free over winter.

planting.note: Sow in place after frost for a tall, quick-blooming stand.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in lean soil and thin to give each plant room to branch. This classic tall blend opens large single flowers in shades of white, pink, and rose atop feathery foliage. Cut stems freely for the vase, which only encourages more buds through the season.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors a few weeks early for earlier bloom.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the bed moist until seedlings emerge. This tall selection carries pure white single blooms that glow at dusk and suit a white or cottage garden. Remove spent flowers regularly and enjoy long-stemmed cuts indoors.

planting.note: Sow outdoors when the soil warms in late spring.

directions: Sow shallowly into unimproved soil and avoid rich fertilizer for the best flowering. Tall stems bear soft rose-pink single daisies that draw bees and butterflies. Keep picking or deadheading to hold the bloom into fall.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last spring frost.

directions: Cover seed lightly and thin the seedlings once they have a few true leaves. This tall type opens deep crimson-rose single flowers that intensify the border. Cut or deadhead often to extend the show through summer.

planting.note: Sow in place once frost has passed.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in poor-to-average soil and keep watering light. Blooms are rose-pink with a deeper crimson eye that makes each daisy stand out. Deadhead spent heads to keep the plant flowering freely.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors for containers.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to give the compact plants light and air. This early dwarf blooms weeks ahead of tall types with clean white single daisies on tidy mounds. Excellent in pots and front borders where no staking is needed.

planting.note: Sow when soil warms, or transplant started plants.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil and thin seedlings early. Compact bushy plants carry soft pink single flowers with a rosy center and need no support. Pinch tips once for extra branching and pick blooms often.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm ground.

directions: Barely cover the seed and thin plants to a hand's width apart. Rich carmine-red single daisies sit on short, sturdy stems that hold up in wind and containers. Deadhead to keep the compact mounds covered in bloom.

planting.note: Sow in place after the frost date.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin the tall seedlings for good airflow. The novelty of this mix is its rolled, fluted petals that form little tubes in white, pink, and rose. Cut freely for arrangements, where the shell-shaped petals draw comment.

planting.note: Direct sow when frost danger is past.

directions: Cover seed lightly in lean soil and keep feeding to a minimum. Each white single bloom is edged with a crimson picotee band, and the pattern varies plant to plant. Deadhead often and cut stems for the vase to prolong flowering.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once soil is warm in late spring.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to give plants room to bush out. White petals are striped and flecked with crimson-pink, so each flower has its own pattern. Keep cutting and deadheading to hold the display into autumn.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors for early color.

directions: Barely cover the seed and thin the compact plants to a foot apart. Flowers open a deep ruby red and fade to antique rose and bronze as they age, giving a range of tones at once. Deadhead lightly and cut stems for striking arrangements.

planting.note: Sow in place after frost for a tall cutting patch.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin tall seedlings for sturdy stems. This selection produces fully double and semi-double pompon flowers in rich rose-pink. A superb cut flower, so harvest stems just as buds open to keep new ones coming.

planting.note: Direct sow when the soil has warmed after frost.

directions: Cover seed lightly and thin plants to reduce crowding. Tall stems carry double and semi-double white flowers with a fluffy, ruffled look. Cut for bouquets and deadhead to encourage a long run of bloom.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin the tall seedlings for air movement. Deep magenta-red double and semi-double flowers make a bold statement in the border and vase. Harvest stems young and keep deadheading for continuous bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover seed in lean soil and thin the seedlings. Large semi-double white flowers show extra ruffled petals around the golden center. Deadhead and cut regularly to keep the tall plants in bloom.

planting.note: Sow in place after frost, or start indoors a few weeks early.

directions: Cover seed lightly and thin plants to give them room. The unusual color blends soft pale yellow with an apricot-pink flush and a deeper rosy eye. Deadhead often and cut stems to prolong this soft, distinctive bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors for containers.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin the compact plants for tidy mounds. This breakthrough dwarf is the first soft primrose-yellow garden cosmos and needs no staking. Pinch once and deadhead to keep the pale lemon flowers coming.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once soil warms after frost.

directions: Barely cover seed and thin the tall seedlings for strong stems. Rich burgundy-red petals are striped and edged in white, so no two flowers match exactly. Deadhead and cut freely to keep the striking blooms coming.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin to reduce crowding. Nearly white flowers carry a soft pink blush that deepens to a rose eye at the center. Keep deadheading and cutting to extend the airy display into fall.

planting.note: Sow in place after frost into warm soil.

directions: Cover seed lightly and thin the tall seedlings for air movement. Some flowers have fused petals that form a charming cupped, cupcake-like shape in soft blush and white. Deadhead and cut for the vase to keep the novelty blooms coming.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors early.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin plants for good airflow. Semi-double and double flowers show a frothy ruffled center with rose-edged white petals. Deadhead and cut stems to keep this playful bloom in production.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after frost for a cut-flower row.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin for strong, straight stems. Bred for the cut-flower trade, this strain gives long sturdy stems and large deep-rose single blooms with a crimson eye. Harvest stems just as buds open and keep cutting for a steady supply.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors for pots.

directions: Barely cover the seed and thin the compact plants. Flowers open a deep salmon-red and age through antique bronze and gold, so a plant shows several tones at once. Deadhead lightly to keep the shifting colors coming on tidy mounds.

planting.note: Sow after frost, or start indoors for early bloom in containers.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil and thin the compact plants. This award-winning dwarf blooms early with vivid orange semi-double flowers on wind-tolerant mounds. Deadhead to keep the neat, heat-proof plants flowering all summer.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors for pots.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and thin the seedlings. Bright golden-yellow semi-double flowers cover these compact, drought-tolerant plants. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and enjoy how well it holds up in heat and containers.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after frost into warm ground.

directions: Barely cover seed and thin the compact plants for airflow. Fiery orange-red semi-double flowers sit above lacy foliage on tidy, heat-loving mounds. Deadhead often to keep the vivid color coming through summer.

planting.note: Sow in place after frost for a hot-colored stand.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in warm soil and thin the seedlings. This popular blend gives semi-double flowers in gold, orange, and scarlet on drought-tolerant plants. Deadhead to keep the bright, butterfly-friendly show going until frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors for edging and pots.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and thin the very compact plants. This dwarf stays low and bushy with rich scarlet-red single flowers, ideal for edging and containers. Deadhead to keep the little plants covered in bloom.

planting.note: Sow after frost, or start indoors for early pots.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil and thin the compact seedlings. Golden-yellow single flowers sit close over the foliage on very tidy dwarf plants. No staking is needed, and steady deadheading keeps them blooming.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once the soil is warm after frost.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in warm ground and thin the seedlings. This heat-loving strain gives semi-double flowers in gold, orange, and red on taller plants. Deadhead to prolong the vivid, drought-tough display into fall.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin the seedlings for airflow. Fiery orange-red semi-double flowers glow above lacy foliage on vigorous, drought-tolerant plants. Deadhead to keep the hot color coming right up to frost.

planting.note: Sow in place after frost for a warm-toned mix.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in warm soil and thin the plants. This semi-tall blend offers semi-double flowers in gold, orange, and scarlet on tough, heat-loving stems. Deadhead often to keep the plants blooming through the summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers or set out started plants once nights are reliably warm.

directions: Set the tuber a few inches deep in rich, well-drained soil and keep watering light until top growth appears. This compact selection bears deep maroon-brown flowers with a distinctive chocolate scent, superb in pots. Lift and store the tubers frost-free over winter in cold climates.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after the last spring frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the same depth they grew in the pot and water in well. This compact selection needs little pinching and holds a tidy mound in containers or borders. Bright red-orange cigar flowers cover the plant nonstop through the heat of summer.

planting.note: Transplant after frost when the ground has warmed.

directions: Set rooted plants into loose, well-drained soil and mulch lightly to hold moisture. It thrives in heat and needs no deadheading to stay in bloom. Look for the vivid scarlet bat-face flowers with dark purple ears all through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant out once frost is past and soil is warm.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings level with the surrounding soil and water in gently. This low, spreading form is happy trailing over the edge of a pot or basket. The red-and-purple flowers resemble little mouse faces and appear steadily from early summer until cold weather.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into well-drained soil and water regularly while they establish. This selection forms a neat, mounded shrublet good for edging and low massing. Small rosy-lavender flowers dot the ferny foliage continuously through the warm season.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Plant rooted starts at their original depth and keep soil evenly moist as they root out. This improved strain carries oversized magenta blooms on a compact, well-branched frame. Flowers stud the fine green foliage from spring until frost with no deadheading needed.

planting.note: Start indoors early or plant out after frost; it blooms best late in the season.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm mix, or set out divisions in spring, giving each plant room to bush out. Grow in full sun with regular water and it will build into a large, arching clump by late summer. The tubular flowers shade from yellow tips to orange bases like candy corn and peak in fall, feeding migrating hummingbirds.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after the last frost once soil is warm.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in full sun and rich, well-drained soil, watering steadily through the heat. This vigorous hybrid grows into a shrubby mound and is a magnet for hummingbirds. Orange tubular flowers flared with a bit of red bloom heavily from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant out after frost danger in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Set rooted plants at pot depth and water in; they need no deadheading to stay in flower. This heat-loving selection is exceptionally floriferous and shrugs off drought once established. A steady supply of orange firecracker blooms keeps hummingbirds visiting from spring until frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter inch deep in warm soil and keep moist until seedlings emerge. This North American native annual has sticky, glandular stems and grows easily in full sun. Small purple flowers appear from midsummer into fall and give way to seed you can save for next year.

planting.note: Start indoors a few weeks early or sow outdoors after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed in warm mix and keep it bright and moist until germination. Grow in full sun in average soil and stake taller plants if they flop. Dusky red-purple tubular flowers rise on upright stems from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Set rooted plants at pot depth and give them a sunny, sheltered spot with good drainage. Part of a compact series bred for baskets and containers, it stays neat without pinching. Bright bat-faced flowers in warm red tones bloom heavily all summer.

planting.note: Transplant after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into loose soil and keep evenly watered while establishing. This selection has a mounded, well-branched habit suited to mixed containers. Cheerful red bat-faced flowers with dark ears carry on from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a warm, sunny location.

directions: Plant rooted starts level with the soil and water them in well. Bred for tidy, uniform growth, it performs well in the heat with little care. Vibrant bat-faced blooms appear steadily through the summer months.

planting.note: Plant out after frost once nights are reliably warm.

directions: Set rooted plants into well-drained soil or containers and water in gently. This heat-tolerant selection blooms very early and needs no deadheading. Rosy-pink flowers cover the tidy mound from late spring until frost.

planting.note: Transplant after frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at their original depth and keep soil lightly moist as they establish. It forms a compact, mounding plant that thrives in summer heat and humidity. Deep violet-purple flowers bloom continuously from spring through frost.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost in a sunny spot.

directions: Plant rooted starts into well-drained soil and water in to settle the roots. This selection has an upright, bushy habit that fills containers quickly. Soft lavender bat-faced flowers appear nonstop through the warm season.

planting.note: Transplant after frost once the soil warms.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at pot depth and keep evenly moist while they root out. Vigorous and free-branching, it holds up well in heat and containers. Rich red bat-faced flowers with dark ears bloom from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors early or set out transplants once frost danger is gone.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of warm mix and keep bright and moist until it sprouts. Grow in full sun with regular water and it builds a loose, shrubby mound. The small tubular flowers glow in orange, yellow, and pink tones and draw hummingbirds from summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings and keep soil evenly moist while they establish. This selection makes a dense, fine-textured shrublet good for low hedging and edging. Small rosy-lilac flowers cover the glossy foliage from spring through fall.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost in sun to light shade.

directions: Plant rooted starts at pot depth and water regularly as they settle in. This white-flowered form pairs well with brighter bedding plants and softens container edges. A steady dusting of tiny white blooms covers the ferny foliage all season.

planting.note: Transplant after frost once nights stay warm.

directions: Set rooted plants into well-drained soil and water them in gently. This compact firecracker type carries loads of narrow tubular blooms with little care. Fiery red-orange flowers cover the mound through summer and attract hummingbirds.

planting.note: Start seed indoors about six weeks before the last frost and set plants out once soil warms.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm trays and transplant seedlings after all frost danger passes. Space plants in rich, well-drained soil and stake the taller stems as they grow. Lift and store the tubers over winter in cold regions, and expect the first blooms by midsummer that continue until frost.

planting.note: Raise from seed indoors and plant out after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm until sprouts appear in a week or two. Move seedlings to a sunny bed with fertile soil and give tall stems support in windy spots. Single scarlet-to-orange flowers open by midsummer and draw pollinators until autumn frost.

planting.note: Start indoors in early spring for compact plants that bloom the first season.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm trays and prick out seedlings once they have a couple of true leaves. Harden off and plant in containers or the front of a bed after frost. This compact double series flowers quickly from seed and stays neat without staking, blooming until fall.

planting.note: Start seed under cover in spring; grown as a bedding annual for its bronze foliage.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm compost and keep evenly moist until germination. Set out hardened seedlings after the last frost in full sun. Dark bronze leaves set off the mixed double flowers, which appear by midsummer and last through the season.

planting.note: An old single-flowered bedding strain raised easily from seed each spring.

directions: Sow indoors a quarter inch deep and transplant when the weather settles and soil warms. Plant in a sunny, fertile bed and pinch the tips once to bush out the plants. Bright single blooms in mixed colors open by midsummer and feed bees until the first frost.

planting.note: A classic dwarf bedding mix grown from seed for first-year color.

directions: Start seed warm in early spring and grow on until frosts have passed. Plant into rich, well-drained soil in full sun and water in dry spells. Semi-double flowers in a wide color range bloom from midsummer to frost and reward regular deadheading.

planting.note: Seed-raised strain from 'Bishop of Llandaff' with dark foliage and jewel colors.

directions: Sow warm indoors and pot on seedlings before planting out after frost. Give the plants full sun and fertile soil to bring out their bronze-black leaves. Single and semi-double flowers in scarlet, orange, and pink open by midsummer and continue until frost.

planting.note: A short double-flowered bedding mixture grown from seed each year.

directions: Barely cover the seed in warm trays and transplant seedlings once frost has passed. Plant in full sun near the front of a border or in pots. Compact plants carry double flowers in mixed colors from midsummer through fall without needing stakes.

planting.note: Seed strain producing collarette flowers with a contrasting inner ruff.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warmth and grow seedlings on until frost has passed. Set plants in a sunny bed with good drainage and pinch once for bushiness. Open-centered blooms with a frilled collar around the disc bloom from midsummer and attract bees.

planting.note: A seed mixture giving quilled, spiky petals in varied colors.

directions: Start the fine seed indoors and transplant after the frost date into rich soil. Give the taller plants a stake or a supporting ring as they grow. Rolled, pointed petals form star-like flowers from midsummer that are excellent for cutting.

planting.note: Seed strain of small, tightly rolled ball-shaped flowers.

directions: Sow warm and grow seedlings on before planting out into a sunny, fertile spot. Support the stems and deadhead spent flowers to keep blooming. Round, honeycomb-petaled pompon flowers appear from midsummer and last well in a vase.

planting.note: Plant tubers after the last frost, or start them early indoors.

directions: Set the tuber about four inches deep with the old stem base pointing up in fertile, well-drained soil. Support the dark stems and water freely once growth is strong. Scarlet single flowers glow against near-black foliage from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Plant tubers once soil has warmed after the last frost.

directions: Plant the tuber a few inches deep in a warm, sunny bed with rich soil. Keep it evenly watered and stake as needed once flowering begins. Deep velvety red single flowers over dark foliage bloom from midsummer until frost.

planting.note: Set tubers out after frost, or start indoors for earlier bloom.

directions: Bury the tuber about four inches deep in fertile soil and full sun. Water regularly and deadhead to keep new buds forming. Golden-yellow single flowers stand out against bronze foliage from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost; a favorite large-flowered cut dahlia.

directions: Plant the tuber four to six inches deep in rich soil and stake it early. Water deeply and feed through the season for the biggest blooms. Huge creamy blush dinnerplate flowers open from late summer and are prized for cutting.

planting.note: Set tubers out after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Plant the tuber several inches deep in full sun and stake the stems. Keep the soil moist and cut flowers often to encourage more buds. Dark maroon-red decorative blooms appear from midsummer and last until frost.

planting.note: A Karma-series cut dahlia; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Set the tuber four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and support it. Water steadily and cut stems regularly for long vase life. Deep chocolate-red decorative flowers over dark foliage bloom from summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost; noted for orange blooms over dark leaves.

directions: Bury the tuber a few inches deep in a sunny, rich bed and stake it. Water well through summer and deadhead spent flowers. Warm apricot-orange decorative blooms contrast with bronze foliage from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: A large purple decorative; plant tubers after the last frost.

directions: Plant the tuber four to six inches deep in fertile soil and stake early. Feed and water generously for full-sized flowers. Rich royal-purple dinnerplate blooms open from late summer and make bold cut flowers.

planting.note: Compact dark-leaved single dahlia; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Set the tuber a few inches deep in full sun with good drainage. Keep watered and deadhead to prolong bloom. Single yellow flowers flushed with orange centers glow over bronze foliage from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: White single flowers over near-black leaves; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber four inches deep in a warm, sunny bed and support it. Water regularly and remove faded flowers. Pure white single blooms shine against dark chocolate foliage from midsummer until frost.

planting.note: Open star-shaped dahlia beloved by bees; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Set the tuber several inches deep in full sun and fertile soil. Water through dry spells and stake taller stems. Widely spaced yellow petals form open star flowers from midsummer that pollinators love, blooming until frost.

planting.note: Pink semi-double over dark foliage; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber a few inches deep in a sunny, well-drained spot. Keep the soil moist and deadhead often. Lilac-pink semi-double flowers contrast with dark purple leaves from midsummer to frost, staying compact for borders and pots.

planting.note: Mystic-series single dahlia with dark foliage; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Set the tuber four inches deep in full sun with rich soil. Water regularly and this floriferous variety needs little staking. Bright yellow single flowers over near-black leaves bloom nonstop from summer to frost.

planting.note: A red-flowered Mystic-series single; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber several inches deep in a sunny, fertile bed. Keep evenly watered and deadhead spent blooms. Red single flowers with warm centers over dark foliage carry on from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Happy Single series single dahlia; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Set the tuber four inches deep in full sun and water in dry weather. Deadhead to keep the compact plant blooming. Orange single flowers with red-tinted centers over dark foliage flower from summer until frost.

planting.note: Happy Single series with a dark eye ring; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber a few inches deep in a warm, sunny position. Water regularly and remove faded flowers to prolong bloom. Pink-and-cream single flowers with a dark central ring bloom over bronze foliage until frost.

planting.note: Gallery series patio dahlia; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Set the tuber a few inches deep in a pot or bed in full sun. Water steadily and deadhead to keep blooms coming. Fully double orange-bronze flowers cover the short, sturdy plants from summer to frost with no staking needed.

planting.note: Gallery series compact patio dahlia; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber a few inches deep in rich, well-drained soil in full sun. Water regularly and deadhead the spent flowers. Double blooms in warm apricot and salmon tones smother the dwarf plants from summer to frost.

planting.note: Gallery series patio dahlia in soft yellow; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Set the tuber a few inches deep in full sun in a container or border front. Keep it watered and remove faded flowers. Soft yellow double blooms crowd the compact plants from summer through frost.

planting.note: Gallery series patio dahlia in pink; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber a few inches deep in fertile soil and full sun. Water steadily and deadhead through the season. Rounded pink double flowers cover these short bushy plants from summer to frost.

planting.note: Gallery series patio dahlia; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Set the tuber a few inches deep in full sun with good drainage. Water regularly and pinch off spent blooms. Creamy white and blush double flowers top the compact plants from summer through frost.

planting.note: A dwarf single bedding dahlia; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Plant the small tuber a few inches deep in full sun near the front of a bed. Water in dry spells and deadhead to keep blooming. Bright single flowers in red and white top these very compact plants from summer to frost.

planting.note: Miniature single bedding dahlia series; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Set the small tuber a few inches deep in a sunny, well-drained spot. Water regularly and remove faded flowers. Masses of small single blooms in clear colors cover the low mounding plants from summer to frost.

planting.note: Very compact patio-pot dahlia series; plant tubers or plants after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber a couple of inches deep in a container of free-draining potting mix. Keep it in full sun, water often, and deadhead regularly. Fully double flowers in many colors cover the tiny plants from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Compact border-and-pot dahlia series; plant tubers after frost.

directions: Set the tuber a few inches deep in full sun and fertile soil. Water steadily and pinch off old flowers to extend bloom. Double flowers in soft pastel shades cover the tidy plants from summer through frost.

planting.note: A bronze-leaved bedding seed strain grown fresh each spring.

directions: Sow warm indoors and grow the seedlings on until frost has passed. Plant into a sunny bed with rich soil and water in dry weather. Semi-double and double flowers in bright colors sit over dark bronze foliage from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Transplant around the last frost into full sun.

directions: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks ahead and keep the mix warm and just moist until sprouts appear. This compact hybrid tolerates both cool spring nights and summer heat better than an old-fashioned China pink, so it holds up in beds and containers. Shear the plant back lightly after the first flush of deep-red flowers to trigger a fresh round of bloom.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors near the last frost date.

directions: Sow indoors and cover lightly, holding the flat at about 68 degrees until germination. This vigorous series carries clean white flowers on tidy, heat-tolerant mounds that perform well in spring and again in fall. Remove faded flowers regularly and water at the base to keep the foliage dry and disease-free.

planting.note: Set out around the last frost in full sun.

directions: Start seed indoors about seven weeks before planting out and keep it lightly covered and warm. Plants form neat mounds topped with rose-pink flowers marked by a deep raspberry eye, blooming early and holding well into warm weather. Deadhead as blossoms fade to keep the compact plants tidy and flowering.

planting.note: Transplant near the last frost; enjoys cool nights.

directions: Sow indoors and expect somewhat uneven germination, so start extra seed and keep the flat evenly moist. This award-winning selection is prized for flowers that vary from cherry-red to lavender, sometimes on the same plant. Give it full sun and well-drained soil, and pinch off old blooms to prolong the changing color show.

planting.note: Plant out around the last frost date.

directions: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before setting out, barely covering it. This dwarf strain produces unusually large single flowers in a mix of pinks and reds, many with a contrasting eye, held just above short foliage. Deadhead often and grow in gritty, well-drained soil to keep the low mounds flowering through cool weather.

planting.note: Set plants out near the last frost.

directions: Sow indoors about six weeks before planting and keep the flat around 68 degrees. This series flowers very early on compact, uniform plants, making it a reliable choice for spring bedding and pots. Provide full sun and steady moisture, and remove spent flowers to keep the crimson blooms coming.

planting.note: Transplant around the last frost in full sun.

directions: Start seed indoors and cover very lightly, keeping the soil warm and moist until seedlings emerge. Plants bear large, fringed flowers in a rich crimson on well-branched mounds that stand up in the garden and in containers. Water at soil level and deadhead regularly to encourage continued bloom.

planting.note: Set out near the last frost date.

directions: Sow indoors six weeks before planting, covering the seed only slightly. This dwarf, early series forms compact cushions smothered in single flowers across a range of pinks, reds, and whites. Grow in full sun with good drainage and shear lightly after the first flush to refresh the display.

planting.note: Plant out around the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors or sow directly once the soil warms, barely covering it. This low, spreading strain hugs the ground and blankets itself with bright single flowers, making it useful for edging and rock gardens. Trim faded blooms and keep the soil well drained to prevent crown rot.

planting.note: Transplant near the last frost in full sun.

directions: Sow indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost, keeping the flat warm and lightly moist. This old award-winning dwarf carries white flowers with a bright scarlet central zone on compact, tidy plants. Deadhead often and give it full sun and sharp drainage for the longest bloom.

planting.note: Set out around the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors about seven weeks before planting, covering it lightly. This award winner forms a compact mound of large rose-pink flowers each with a deep red eye, holding up well in heat. Water at the base and remove faded blooms to keep the plants covered in flower.

planting.note: Transplant near the last frost in full sun.

directions: Sow indoors and press the seed lightly into the surface, keeping it warm until it sprouts. This compact companion to Strawberry Parfait bears pale pink flowers with a bold raspberry-red center. Give it full sun and good drainage, and deadhead through the season to prolong the show.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost for summer-long bloom.

directions: Start seed indoors seven to eight weeks before setting out and keep the mix warm. Unlike most pinks, this heat-loving hybrid holds its bright pink flower clusters right through summer without stalling. Give it full sun, room to reach knee height, and an occasional shearing to spark repeat bloom.

planting.note: Transplant around the last frost; tolerates both heat and cold.

directions: Sow indoors about eight weeks before planting out and grow the seedlings on cool and bright. This vigorous cut-flower type sends up tall stems topped with dense magenta-purple clusters that last well in the vase. Cut stems frequently or deadhead to keep new flowering shoots coming from spring into summer.
